message:
📝 docs(security): simplify mermaid diagrams to fix github render truncation

Signed-off-by: Andrew Anderson <andy@clubanderson.com>

files.patch:
@@ -18,51 +18,23 @@ The three-process architecture: a browser, a Go backend (serves UI, bootstrap-on ```mermaid flowchart LR - subgraph User["User machine"] - B[Browser] - KA[kc-agent
127.0.0.1:8585
loopback only] - KC[~/.kube/config] - CFG[~/.kc/config.yaml
AI keys, mode 0600] - end - - subgraph Console["Console deployment
(self-hosted cluster or SaaS)"] - GB[Go backend
:8080] - POD[(Pod SA)] - end - - subgraph Clusters["Your managed clusters"] - K8S[Kubernetes API servers] - end - - subgraph AI["AI providers (optional)"] - ANTH[Anthropic] - OAI[OpenAI] - GEM[Gemini] - GRQ[Groq / OpenRouter /
Open WebUI] - end - - B -->|HTTP/WS :8080
serves UI, OAuth| GB - B -->|HTTP/WS :8585
all cluster ops| KA - KA -->|reads on disk| KC - KA -->|reads on disk| CFG - KA -->|kubectl as
user identity| K8S - KA -.->|HTTPS chat
prompts only| ANTH - KA -.->|HTTPS chat
prompts only| OAI - KA -.->|HTTPS chat
prompts only| GEM - KA -.->|HTTPS chat
prompts only| GRQ - GB -->|bootstrap /
GPU reserve /
self-upgrade ONLY| POD - - classDef userbox fill:#0b3d0b,stroke:#0f0,color:#fff - classDef consolebox fill:#0a2a4a,stroke:#08f,color:#fff - classDef clusterbox fill:#3d0b0b,stroke:#f44,color:#fff - classDef aibox fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#fa0,color:#fff - class User userbox - class Console consolebox - class Clusters clusterbox - class AI aibox + B[Browser] --> GB[Backend] + B --> KA[kc-agent] + KA --> KC[kubeconfig] + KA --> CFG[AI keys] + KA --> K8S[Kubernetes] + KA -.-> AI[AI provider] + GB --> POD[Pod SA] ``` -Dashed lines are optional: AI provider calls only happen when a key is configured. Solid lines with arrows are mandatory for full cluster-management functionality. +**Legend:** + +- **Browser → Backend** — UI on port 8080 (browser-side OAuth optional). +- **Browser → kc-agent** — every cluster operation, on `127.0.0.1:8585` (loopback only). kc-agent reads `~/.kube/config` and stores AI keys at `~/.kc/config.yaml` (mode `0600`). +- **kc-agent → Kubernetes** — uses the user's own kubeconfig identity. Per-cluster RBAC is enforced by each apiserver against the user, never against the console's pod SA. +- **kc-agent ⤳ AI provider** — dashed: only happens when an API key is configured. Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, Groq, OpenRouter, Open WebUI, or any local OpenAI-compatible endpoint via `GROQ_BASE_URL` / `OPENROUTER_BASE_URL` / `OPEN_WEBUI_URL`. +- **Backend → Pod SA** — bootstrap-only — serving the frontend, GPU reservation, and self-upgrade. The backend never acts on a managed cluster. +- **Solid arrows** are mandatory for the core cluster-management UX. **Dashed arrows** to AI providers are optional and only used when an API key is configured. ### Who is who @@ -120,32 +92,22 @@ If you need to audit what leaves the machine, the provider files under `pkg/agen ```mermaid flowchart LR - RB[Remote browser
any other LAN host] - LB[Local browser
127.0.0.1] - - subgraph Gates["kc-agent defense in depth"] - direction TB - BIND[Bind check
127.0.0.1 only
server.go:578] - CORS[Origin allow-list
strict match
server.go:92-100] - REB[DNS-rebinding guard
matchOrigin
server.go:799-822] - TOK["Token check
(optional, off by default)
server.go:214-218"] - KA[kc-agent handlers
kubectl via user kubeconfig] - end - - RB -.rejected at OS
socket layer.-> BIND - LB --> BIND - BIND --> CORS - CORS --> REB - REB --> TOK - TOK --> KA - - classDef rejected stroke:#f44,stroke-width:2px - classDef allowed stroke:#0f0,stroke-width:2px - class RB rejected - class LB,BIND,CORS,REB,TOK,KA allowed + RB[Remote] -.X.-> BIND[Bind] + LB[Local] --> BIND + BIND --> CORS[CORS] + CORS --> REB[Rebind] + REB --> TOK[Token] + TOK --> KA[Handlers] ``` -The loopback bind is the primary defense against network-level access. The CORS allow-list, DNS-rebinding guard, and optional token are layered defenses against local attackers — rogue browser tabs or other local processes that could reach `127.0.0.1:8585` if loopback alone were the only gate. Setting `KC_AGENT_TOKEN` adds the fourth layer, which is recommended when the user cannot assume that all local processes are trusted. +Four layers gate every request to kc-agent (`pkg/agent/server.go`): + +1. **Bind check** (`server.go:578`) — kc-agent listens on `127.0.0.1:8585` only. Remote LAN hosts are rejected at the OS socket layer. +2. **CORS allow-list** (`server.go:92-100`) — strict origin matching for browser requests. +3. **DNS-rebinding guard** (`matchOrigin`, `server.go:799-822`) — defends against attacker-controlled DNS resolving to `127.0.0.1`. +4. **Token check** (`server.go:214-218`) — optional shared secret. Set `KC_AGENT_TOKEN` for this fourth layer, recommended when you cannot assume all local processes are trusted. + +The loopback bind is the primary defense against network-level access. The CORS allow-list, DNS-rebinding guard, and optional token are layered defenses against local attackers — rogue browser tabs or other local processes that could reach `127.0.0.1:8585` if loopback alone were the only gate. ### What actually leaves the cluster (when self-hosted in-cluster) @@ -187,38 +149,36 @@ Card proxies that call third-party APIs (ArgoCD, Prometheus, etc.) are only used ### Posture comparison +**Posture A — fully online (default):** everything enabled. + ```mermaid -flowchart TB - subgraph A["Posture A — fully online (default)"] - direction LR - A_KA[kc-agent] --> A_K8S[Kubernetes] - A_KA --> A_AI[Public AI provider] - A_GB[Go backend] --> A_GH[GitHub OAuth] - A_GB --> A_UP[Update checks] - end - - subgraph B["Posture B — restricted egress (no AI)"] - direction LR - B_KA[kc-agent] --> B_K8S[Kubernetes] - B_KA -.blocked.-> B_AI[Public AI provider] - B_GB[Go backend] --> B_GH[GitHub OAuth] - end - - subgraph C["Posture C — fully air-gapped"] - direction LR - C_KA[kc-agent] --> C_K8S[Kubernetes] - C_KA --> C_LLM[Local LLM
optional] - C_KA -.blocked.-> C_AI[Public AI provider] - C_GB[Go backend] -.blocked.-> C_GH[GitHub OAuth] - end - - classDef allowed stroke:#0f0,stroke-width:2px - classDef blocked stroke:#f44,stroke-width:2px,color:#f88 - class A_KA,A_K8S,A_AI,A_GB,A_GH,A_UP,B_KA,B_K8S,B_GB,B_GH,C_KA,C_K8S,C_LLM allowed - class B_AI,C_AI,C_GH blocked +flowchart LR + A_KA[kc-agent] --> A_K8S[Kubernetes] + A_KA --> A_AI[Public LLM] + A_GB[Backend] --> A_GH[GitHub OAuth] + A_GB --> A_UP[Update checks] +``` + +**Posture B — restricted egress, no AI:** all cluster-management features still work. + +```mermaid +flowchart LR + B_KA[kc-agent] --> B_K8S[Kubernetes] + B_KA -.blocked.-> B_AI[Public LLM] + B_GB[Backend] --> B_GH[GitHub OAuth] +``` + +**Posture C — fully air-gapped:** no public AI, no GitHub OAuth, optional local LLM. + +```mermaid +flowchart LR + C_KA[kc-agent] --> C_K8S[Kubernetes] + C_KA --> C_LLM[Local LLM] + C_KA -.blocked.-> C_AI[Public LLM] + C_GB[Backend] -.blocked.-> C_GH[GitHub OAuth] ``` -Dotted arrows are explicitly blocked at the egress (firewall or network policy). Every arrow that remains is an outbound call that must succeed for the feature on that arrow to work. +Dotted arrows are explicitly blocked at the egress (firewall or network policy). ### What must exist inside your perimeter @@ -254,17 +214,12 @@ Note the asymmetry: **the upstream OpenAI provider does not currently honor an ` ```mermaid flowchart LR - KA[kc-agent] -->|provider: groq
GROQ_BASE_URL=...| SLOT[Groq provider slot
pkg/agent/provider_groq.go] - SLOT -.default.-> GRQ[api.groq.com/v1] - SLOT -->|override| OLLA[Ollama
localhost:11434/v1] - SLOT -->|override| VLLM[vLLM
local:8000/v1] - SLOT -->|override| LM[LM Studio
local:1234/v1] - SLOT -->|override| GW[Corporate LLM gateway
llm.internal/v1] - - classDef default_path stroke:#888,stroke-width:1px - classDef override_path stroke:#0f0,stroke-width:2px - class GRQ default_path - class OLLA,VLLM,LM,GW override_path + KA[kc-agent] --> SLOT[Groq slot] + SLOT -.default.-> GRQ[api.groq.com] + SLOT --> OLLA[Ollama] + SLOT --> VLLM[vLLM] + SLOT --> LM[LM Studio] + SLOT --> GW[Corporate gateway] ``` Setting `GROQ_BASE_URL` redirects every chat-completion call made by the Groq provider to your own endpoint. The request payload is unchanged — it's the OpenAI wire format — so any OpenAI-compatible local runner works without the console knowing or caring which one.
